question: Where do the chemical elements come from?

options: H, He, and a little Li from Big Bang nucleosynthesis; elements up to iron from stellar fusion (and supernovae); roughly half the elements beyond iron from the slow (s) neutron-capture process in AGB stars, the rest from the rapid (r) process in explosive sites like neutron-star mergers
All elements were made in the Big Bang (this confuses two distinct concepts) (not supported by the evidence) (a common misconception) (this reverses the actual relationship)
All elements heavier than helium are made only in the Sun (an outdated view superseded by later work) (inconsistent with standard results in the field)
Heavy elements form by fusion beyond iron in ordinary stars (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification) (true only as a special case, not in general)
passive.explanation: The cosmic origin of elements is multi-source. Big Bang nucleosynthesis (first minutes) made $^1$H, $^4$He (~25% by mass), and traces of D, $^3$He, $^7$Li. Stellar fusion built C, N, O up to the iron peak (where binding energy peaks, so fusion stops yielding energy). Elements heavier than iron cannot form by charged-particle fusion; they are made by neutron capture: the s-process (slow, between beta decays) in asymptotic-giant-branch stars makes ~half, while the r-process (rapid, neutron-rich) makes the rest in explosive, neutron-dense environments. The 1957 B$^2$FH paper (Burbidge, Burbidge, Fowler, Hoyle) laid out this framework, famously summarised as 'we are made of star stuff'.

active.prompt: Match each nucleosynthesis process with its site/products:

pairs.definition: First ~3 min; makes H, $^4$He (~25%), D, $^3$He, $^7$Li; probes baryon density
H, He burning up to the iron peak; powers stars; triple-alpha makes carbon
Slow neutron capture in AGB stars; follows valley of stability; ~half of A>56
Rapid neutron capture in NS mergers/supernovae; makes gold, platinum, uranium

active.explanation: The two neutron-capture processes differ by timescale relative to beta decay. The s-process adds neutrons slowly, so unstable isotopes decay before capturing again, hugging the stability valley (Sr, Ba, Pb). The r-process adds neutrons faster than decay, pushing far to the neutron-rich side, then beta-decaying back to make the heaviest elements including the actinides. GW170817 confirmed neutron-star mergers as r-process sites.

question: What is the triple-alpha process and why is it remarkable?

options: Helium burning: $3\,^4$He $\to\,^{12}$C, proceeding via the unstable $^8$Be intermediate and a resonant excited state of carbon (the Hoyle state at 7.65 MeV) predicted by Hoyle on anthropic grounds and later confirmed — without it carbon (and life) would be scarce
Three protons fusing into helium (a common misconception) (this conflates cause and effect) (not supported by the evidence) (true only as a special case, not in general)
A process that destroys carbon in stars (this reverses the actual relationship) (an outdated view superseded by later work) (inconsistent with standard results in the field)
Fusion of carbon into three alphas releasing energy (this describes a different phenomenon) (this confuses two distinct concepts) (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification)
passive.explanation: Bridging the mass gaps at A=5 and A=8 (no stable nuclei) requires an unusual route: two alphas form $^8$Be (unstable, lifetime ~$10^{-16}$ s), and before it decays a third alpha is captured. This would be far too slow except that there is a resonance — the Hoyle state, a $0^+$ excited level of $^{12}$C at 7.65 MeV — almost exactly matching the energy of $^8$Be + $^4$He. Hoyle predicted this level must exist because carbon is abundant (a celebrated anthropic/predictive argument); it was confirmed experimentally. Triple-alpha ignites at ~$10^8$ K in red giants and is the gateway to all heavier elements. Its extreme temperature sensitivity ($\propto T^{40}$) makes helium burning sharply thresholded.

question: What does Big Bang nucleosynthesis predict and test?

options: In the first ~3 minutes, the universe synthesised light nuclei; predicted abundances (~25% $^4$He by mass, plus D, $^3$He, $^7$Li) depend on the baryon-to-photon ratio, providing a stringent, independent measure of the cosmic baryon density that agrees with the CMB
It predicts the abundance of iron and uranium (this describes a different phenomenon) (true only as a special case, not in general) (this conflates cause and effect)
It depends on the dark-energy density only (not supported by the evidence) (an outdated view superseded by later work) (a common misconception)
It makes no testable predictions (this reverses the actual relationship) (inconsistent with standard results in the field) (this confuses two distinct concepts)
passive.explanation: Big Bang nucleosynthesis (BBN) is one of cosmology's pillars. As the universe cooled through ~$10^9$ K, protons and neutrons (frozen at a ratio set by the n-p mass difference and weak rates) assembled into D, then $^4$He (capturing essentially all neutrons, giving the robust ~25% helium mass fraction), with small amounts of $^3$He, D, and $^7$Li. The yields depend mainly on one parameter, the baryon-to-photon ratio $\eta$ (equivalently $\Omega_b h^2$). The deuterium abundance is an especially sensitive 'baryometer'. Strikingly, the $\eta$ inferred from BBN agrees with the completely independent value from the CMB acoustic peaks — a triumph for the hot Big Bang. The persistent 'lithium problem' (observed $^7$Li below prediction) is an active puzzle. BBN also limits the number of light neutrino species and exotic physics.

question: How does the s-process work and where does it occur?

options: Slow neutron capture (neutron flux low enough that beta decay usually precedes the next capture), so the path follows the valley of stability; occurs mainly in thermally-pulsing AGB stars with neutrons from $^{13}$C($\alpha,n$) and $^{22}$Ne($\alpha,n$); produces Sr, Ba, Pb and terminates near $^{209}$Bi
Rapid neutron capture making uranium (this describes a different phenomenon) (this reverses the actual relationship) (a common misconception) (this confuses two distinct concepts)
Proton capture in novae (this conflates cause and effect) (not supported by the evidence) (inconsistent with standard results in the field) (true only as a special case, not in general)
Photodisintegration of iron (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification) (an outdated view superseded by later work) (this describes a different phenomenon)
passive.explanation: In the s-process ('slow'), neutron captures are rare enough that any unstable nucleus produced typically beta-decays before capturing another neutron, so the reaction path stays close to the line of stability, stepping along it. Bottlenecks at nuclei with small capture cross-sections (magic neutron numbers) build up abundance peaks at Sr/Y/Zr (N=50), Ba/La (N=82), and Pb (N=126). The main astrophysical site is thermally-pulsing asymptotic-giant-branch (AGB) stars, where neutron sources $^{13}$C($\alpha,n$)$^{16}$O and $^{22}$Ne($\alpha,n$)$^{25}$Mg supply a modest neutron flux; a 'weak' s-process in massive stars makes lighter species. The chain ends near bismuth/polonium where alpha decay cycles material back. Branchings at unstable isotopes act as thermometers/densitometers of the stellar interior. Roughly half of the heavy-element abundances come from the s-process.

question: What confirmed neutron-star mergers as r-process sites?

options: GW170817: the gravitational-wave detection of a binary neutron-star merger plus its electromagnetic 'kilonova', whose reddening light curve matched radioactive decay of freshly-synthesised heavy r-process elements (lanthanides), directly linking such mergers to gold/platinum production
A laboratory experiment making uranium (this conflates cause and effect) (this describes a different phenomenon) (this confuses two distinct concepts)
The discovery of the Higgs boson (this reverses the actual relationship) (not supported by the evidence) (inconsistent with standard results in the field)
Observations of the Sun's spectrum (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification) (an outdated view superseded by later work) (a common misconception)
passive.explanation: The r-process ('rapid') needs an extreme neutron flux so that captures vastly outpace beta decay, driving nuclei far to the neutron-rich side before they decay back to stability, building the heaviest elements (including the actinides like uranium and thorium and the rare-earth/precious metals). Candidate sites long debated between core-collapse supernovae and neutron-star mergers. GW170817 (17 August 2017) settled it for mergers: the gravitational-wave signal of two coalescing neutron stars was accompanied by a kilonova (AT2017gfo) whose thermal emission, reddening over days, and spectral features matched the radioactive heating of newly-made lanthanide-rich r-process ejecta — and the inferred ejected mass implied mergers are a major source of the universe's heavy elements. A spectroscopic identification of strontium followed. This multi-messenger event was a landmark for both gravitational-wave astronomy and nuclear astrophysics.

question: What nuclear-physics inputs does r-process modelling require, and why are they uncertain?

options: Masses, beta-decay rates, neutron-capture cross-sections, and fission yields of thousands of extremely neutron-rich nuclei far from stability — most never measured in the lab — so models rely on theoretical extrapolations whose uncertainties propagate into predicted abundance patterns
Only the mass of the proton is needed (this conflates cause and effect) (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification) (this describes a different phenomenon)
All required nuclei have been measured precisely (a common misconception) (this confuses two distinct concepts) (an outdated view superseded by later work)
No nuclear data are needed, only hydrodynamics (true only as a special case, not in general) (inconsistent with standard results in the field) (this reverses the actual relationship)
passive.explanation: The r-process path threads through very neutron-rich, short-lived nuclei near the neutron drip line, the vast majority of which lie beyond current experimental reach. Models therefore require theoretical nuclear data over huge swaths of the chart: nuclear masses (which set neutron separation energies and the path), beta-decay half-lives (which control the flow toward stability and the timescale), neutron-capture and photodissociation rates (which shape waiting points at the N=50, 82, 126 closed shells producing the abundance peaks), and fission barriers/yields (which recycle the heaviest nuclei and may set the actinide abundances). Because these are extrapolations of nuclear models into unexplored territory, their uncertainties dominate r-process abundance predictions. This drives rare-isotope facilities (FRIB, RIBF, the future at GSI/FAIR) to measure ever more neutron-rich nuclei, anchoring the models. Combined with astrophysical site uncertainties (merger ejecta dynamics, neutrino effects), r-process nucleosynthesis remains a frontier coupling nuclear structure to cosmic chemical evolution.
